content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 7, 2000


Mr. John T. Doughty
Community Development Director
City of Watsonville
215 Union Street, Second Floor
Watsonville, CA 95076-5009

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-194

Dear Mr. Doughty:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Director of the Watsonville Community Development Department under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your questions appear to be general and precautionary in nature, and involve other future conduct not specifically described in your letter. Thus, we are treating these questions as requests for informal assistance. (Reg. 18329(b)(8)(C) and (D).) Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Section 83114; Reg. 18329(c)(3).) Further, please note that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 83114; In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Your spouse is considering employment with Pacific Municipal Consultants, a current city contractor. If you use the methodology described in your letter to shield yourself from making, participating in making, or influencing any governmental decision concerning Pacific Municipal Consultants, will you have any conflicts of interest by virtue of your spouse's employment?


¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. Please note that Commission regulations are currently being amended and therefore the rules described in this letter may change. You may follow the progress of the pending amendments to the conflict of interest regulations on the Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov).

Our File No. I-00-194
Page 2

CONCLUSION

So long as you do not make, participate in making, or influence any decision financially
affecting Pacific Municipal Consultants, you will not have a conflict of interest under the Act
based on your spouse's employment with Pacific Municipal Consultants.

FACTS

You are the Community Development Director for the City of Watsonville. The
Community Development Department (the CDD), on occasion, contracts with planning and
engineering consulting firms for projects such as environmental impact reports and other related
planning tasks. All contracts are subject to approval by the City Manager and those in excess of
$15,000 require City Council approval.

Recently, your wife was offered a position with Pacific Municipal Consultants (PMC) as
a Senior Planner. PMC is a multi-office planning consulting firm that has two outstanding
contracts with the CDD. PMC is also likely to continue to submit proposals for work with the
city in the future.

You propose the following methodology in order to avoid conflicts of interest:

1) The CDD will not enter into any contracts with PMC unless the contract is awarded
through a competitive bid process.

2) The city will create a list of qualified consultants and use this list to solicit proposals for
contract services.

3) Where PMC has submitted a proposal, you will forward all the proposals to the City
Manager. The City Manager or his or her designee will make the decision on the
recommendation for the award of the contract. The city staff will also process the
contract award. You state that neither you nor the CDD staff will be involved in awarding
a contract to PMC.

4) After the contract has been awarded, the CDD will be responsible for contract
management. The City Manager or his or her designee shall address any disputes
regarding payment under the contract or the breach of contract, should any such issues
arise.

Our File No. J-00-194
Page 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the following eight-step analysis. Please note that unless steps one, two, three, five and six are answered in the affirmative, a conflict of interest will not exist.

Step One: Are you a "public official"?

A "public official" is defined to include "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency...." (Section 82048.) As Director of the CDD in Watsonville, you are a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, are a "public official" subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Reg. 18701(a).)

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.3.) We have enclosed regulations 18702.1 - 18704 for your information.

The methodology you describe to avoid conflicts of interest appears to focus on this factor. According to your letter, where PMC submits a proposal on a contract with the city, you will forward all of the proposals submitted in response to the Request for Proposals to the City Manager who will make the decision on the recommendation for award and will process the award accordingly. In this manner you seek to avoid "making" any decision that might

Our File No. I-00-194
Page 4

financially affect PMC. We also assume that you will not "participate in" or "influence" the
contract decision as those terms are defined above. Based on this proposed methodology, you
will not have a conflict of interest with respect to the award of a contract to PMC.²

However, you also propose that your department will manage the contract. Your
involvement in contract monitoring or management may constitute "making, participating in
making, or influencing" a governmental decision as contemplated by the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act. However, if you do not participate in the contract management and do not
influence any management decisions concerning PMC's contract, you will not have a conflict of
interest under the Act. Moreover, your potential conflicts of interest involvement of other CDD
staff so long as you do not make, participate.in making, or influence
the decisions.

Step Three: Does the public official have economic interests?

While, under your proposed methodology, you will not have a conflict of interest based
on our review of step two of the standard conflict of interest analysis; we note what appears to be
some confusion in your letter as to what constitutes an "economic interest" under the Act.

You state that your spouse will not work on any city projects for PMC. However, PMC
is a potentially disqualifying economic interest by virtue of section 87103(c) and regulation
18703.3 (copy enclosed). This is true whether your spouse is working on a city project or not. A
public official has an economic interest in any person (including a business entity) from whom
the official has received income aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
"Income" includes an official's community property interest in the income of his or her spouse.
(Section 82030.) Thus, it is PMC that is the economic interest at issue in your facts.

Of course decisions that affect your spouse's salary are also decisions in which you have
an economic interest. (See, Reg. 18703.5, copy enclosed.) However, your facts do not suggest
that such an economic interest is at issue.

2 You state that the contract will be subject to a competitive bid process. If selection of a contractor were a purely
ministerial act, such that you will have no discretion with respect to the terms of the contract or the acceptance of the
low bidder for the contract, the decision may not be considered a governmental decision which could lead to a
conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation 18702.4(a)(1), copy enclosed.) However, we do not have sufficient
information about the city's bid process to advise on this issue.

Our File No. 1-00-194
Page 5

The conflict of interest analysis under the Act includes five other steps, however, based
on the methodology that you have proposed it is unnecessary for us to analyze these other steps.

If you have any other questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660. We
have enclosed a fact sheet for your information concerning conflicts of interest.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enc: Regs. 18702.1 - 18702.4, 18703.3, and 18703.5.
Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview (June 1999)
